content.assessment: This proposal most directly mirrors Poland's 1999 structural mechanism — delaying first selection via a comprehensive middle tier — and the lens's evidence base speaks most directly to it. The distributional effects Poland recorded (concentrated gains among students previously destined for vocational tracks) support the structural logic of delayed selection reducing sorting-driven inequality. However, the lens's reversibility question is the central threat: Poland's 2017 reversal occurred 'regardless of the reform's substantive merits,' indicating that structural mechanism quality does not guarantee durability. Implementation sequence also matters — the reversal's costs (7,000 school closures, 10,000 teacher job losses, double-cohort overcrowding) show that the mechanism for undoing structural reorganization can itself generate severe disruption independent of the original reform's success.
opportunities: A one-time, well-sequenced implementation could avoid Poland's reversal costs if political durability is secured up front, unlike Poland's case where reversal came after measured success
strengths: Directly replicates the structural mechanism (delayed selection via comprehensive middle tier) that Poland's 1999 reform used, with documented PISA gains concentrated among previously vocational-track students
Distributional effects target exactly the group (would-be vocational-track students) shown to benefit most in the Polish case
threats: Polish outcome estimates and reversal dynamics are not directly portable per the lens's own limitation — political conditions producing the 2016 reversal are country-specific and may not predict Hungarian durability either way
weaknesses: Reversibility risk is not hypothetical but empirically demonstrated: Poland's structurally similar reform was substantially reversed in 2017 despite measured success

Implementation sequence risk is severe if reversal ever occurs — Poland's reversal alone produced closures, job losses, and overcrowding attributable to the act of restructuring itself, not to either structural design
